title: Deddf Llesiant Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol (Cymru) 2015 / Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015
short: Deddf Llesiant Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol / Well-being of Future Generations Act 2015
type: Primary Legislation — Senedd Cymru / Welsh Parliament (formerly National Assembly for Wales)
chapters: 53 adran / sections mewn 5 Rhan / in 5 Parts + 4 Atodlen / Schedules. Internationally recognised as one of the most holistic pieces of sustainable development legislation worldwide. Establishes 7 well-being goals, a sustainable development principle (5 ways of working), a well-being duty on 44+ public bodies, a Future Generations Commissioner, and Public Services Boards. AMENDED by the Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 — which substituted 'FAIR WORK' (gwaith teg) for 'decent work' in the 'Prosperous Wales' goal. This amendment is the STATUTORY BRIDGE between the Well-being framework and employment law in Wales. The SPPP Act's Social Partnership Duty and socially responsible procurement duties are exercised THROUGH the well-being objectives framework established by THIS Act. Understanding this Act is prerequisite to understanding every other Welsh instrument in the KL estate.
bilingualNote: Mae'r Ddeddf hon ar gael yn Gymraeg ac yn Saesneg gyda'r ddau destun yn gyfreithiol gyfartal. / This Act is available in Welsh and English with both texts having equal legal standing. All section references are identical in both language versions. The Welsh text is an original co-equal enactment, not a translation. The 7 well-being goals and 5 ways of working are documented below in both languages.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Bil Llesiant Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol (Cymru) / Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Bill
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Jeff Cuthbert AM, then Carl Sargeant AM (Minister for Natural Resources), Welsh Government, 7 July 2014
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Section 1 and enabling provisions in force from 29 April 2015. Main provisions (Parts 2-4, including the well-being duty, Future Generations Commissioner, and Public Services Boards) in force from 1 April 2016. The Act has been in force for 10 YEARS — it is a mature, embedded framework, not a new experiment.

significantAmendments.instrument: Deddf Partneriaeth Gymdeithasol a Chaffael Cyhoeddus (Cymru) 2023 / Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023
significantAmendments.effect: Section 14 of the SPPP Act amends section 4 of the WFG Act by substituting 'FAIR WORK' (gwaith teg) for 'decent work' (gwaith gweddus) in the description of 'Cymru Lewyrchus / A Prosperous Wales'. This is the single most important amendment to the WFG Act — it creates a statutory bridge between well-being goals and employment law. In force from 1 October 2024.

significantAmendments.instrument: Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014, Planning (Wales) Act 2015, Environment (Wales) Act 2016
significantAmendments.effect: These Acts align with and complement the WFG Act framework. The Planning Act integrates well-being goals into the planning system. The Environment Act addresses the 'Resilient Wales' goal.

precedingDocuments.title: One Wales: One Planet — Sustainable Development Scheme (2009)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor policy framework. The WFG Act puts sustainable development on a STATUTORY footing rather than relying on policy commitments alone.

precedingDocuments.title: United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (2015)
precedingDocuments.significance: The 7 Welsh well-being goals are directly linked to the 17 UN SDGs. Wales is the only country in the world that has legislated to embed the UN's sustainable development framework into domestic law through a dedicated Act.

leadingCases.name: No significant judicial review challenges to date
leadingCases.principle: The WFG Act operates primarily through the Commissioner's advisory powers, the Auditor General's examination powers, and public reporting requirements — not through litigation. The Commissioner can review public bodies and make recommendations that bodies must respond to. Bodies must publish annual reports. This 'soft power' accountability model has been criticised for lacking teeth but has driven significant culture change across Welsh public services.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE: Monitor for any judicial review challenges to public body decisions on the basis of failure to apply the sustainable development principle, particularly as the SPPP Act's procurement provisions create more concrete, actionable obligations that may generate disputes.

parts.title: Rhan 1 / Part 1 — Y Saith Nod Llesiant a'r Egwyddor Datblygu Cynaliadwy / The Seven Well-being Goals and the Sustainable Development Principle

sections.title: Datblygu cynaliadwy, y saith nod, a'r pum ffordd o weithio / Sustainable development, the seven goals, and the five ways of working
sections.summary: The Act establishes sustainable development as the central organising principle for Welsh public services. It defines sustainable development, sets 7 well-being goals that describe the Wales the Act seeks to create, and establishes 5 ways of working that public bodies must follow. The goals and ways of working are the lens through which ALL Welsh public body decisions should be made — including decisions about employment, procurement, and service delivery.
sections.text:
DATBLYGU CYNALIADWY / SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT (s.2):

The Act defines sustainable development as:
'the process of improving the economic, social, environmental and cultural well-being of Wales by taking action, in accordance with the sustainable development principle, aimed at achieving the well-being goals.'

This definition is critical — it is the STATUTORY MANDATE for Welsh public bodies. Every decision, every policy, every procurement must be assessed against this definition.

YR EGWYDDOR DATBLYGU CYNALIADWY / THE SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT PRINCIPLE (s.5):

'A body must act in a manner which seeks to ensure that the needs of the present are met without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.'

This is the Brundtland definition embedded in Welsh law. It requires public bodies to think INTERGENERATIONALLY — not just about today's workforce, but about the workforce of 2050.

Y PUM FFORDD O WEITHIO / THE FIVE WAYS OF WORKING (s.5):

Public bodies must demonstrate that they have applied the sustainable development principle by following five ways of working:

(1) HIR DYMOR / LONG-TERM:
Balancing short-term needs with the need to safeguard the ability to meet long-term needs.
Employment example: Not cutting training budgets for short-term savings when this will undermine workforce capability for the next 20 years.

(2) ATAL / PREVENTION:
How acting to prevent problems occurring or getting worse may help public bodies meet their objectives.
Employment example: Investing in staff well-being programmes to prevent sickness absence, rather than managing absence reactively.

(3) INTEGREIDDIO / INTEGRATION:
Considering how the public body's well-being objectives may impact upon each of the well-being goals, on their objectives, or on the objectives of other public bodies.
Employment example: Recognising that a workforce restructure (Prosperous Wales) may affect staff mental health (Healthier Wales) and community cohesion (Cohesive Communities).

(4) CYDWEITHIO / COLLABORATION:
Acting in collaboration with any other person (or different parts of the body itself) that could help the body to meet its well-being objectives.
Employment example: Working with trade unions, training providers, and other public bodies to develop shared workforce strategies.

(5) CYNNWYS / INVOLVEMENT:
The importance of involving people with an interest in achieving the well-being goals, and ensuring that those people reflect the diversity of the area which the body serves.
Employment example: Meaningfully consulting staff and their representatives when making decisions that affect them — not just informing after the fact.

Y SAITH NOD LLESIANT / THE SEVEN WELL-BEING GOALS (s.4):

(1) CYMRU LEWYRCHUS / A PROSPEROUS WALES:
An innovative, productive and low carbon society which recognises the limits of the global environment and therefore uses resources efficiently and proportionately (including acting on climate change); and which develops a skilled and well-educated population in an economy which generates wealth and provides employment opportunities, allowing people to take advantage of the wealth generated through securing FAIR WORK (gwaith teg).

⚠️ CRITICAL AMENDMENT: 'decent work' was replaced with 'FAIR WORK' by the SPPP Act 2023 s.14, in force from 1 October 2024. This is the statutory bridge between the WFG Act and employment law. Fair work (as defined by the Fair Work Commission 2019): workers are fairly rewarded, heard and represented, secure and able to progress in a healthy, inclusive environment where rights are respected.

(2) CYMRU GYDNERTH / A RESILIENT WALES:
A nation which maintains and enhances a biodiverse natural environment with healthy functioning ecosystems that support social, economic and ecological resilience and the capacity to adapt to change (for example climate change).
Employment relevance: workforce adaptation to climate change, green skills, just transition.

(3) CYMRU IACHACH / A HEALTHIER WALES:
A society in which people's physical and mental well-being is maximised and in which choices and behaviours that benefit future health are understood.
Employment relevance: occupational health, mental health at work, well-being programmes, workload management, stress prevention.

(4) CYMRU SY'N FWY CYFARTAL / A MORE EQUAL WALES:
A society that enables people to fulfil their potential no matter what their background or circumstances (including their socio-economic background and circumstances).
Employment relevance: pay equality, socio-economic duty, diversity and inclusion, access to employment for disadvantaged groups.

(5) CYMRU O GYMUNEDAU CYDLYNUS / A WALES OF COHESIVE COMMUNITIES:
Attractive, viable, safe and well-connected communities.
Employment relevance: local employment, community anchoring, avoiding workforce displacement that fragments communities.

(6) CYMRU Â DIWYLLIANT BYWIOG LLE MAE'R GYMRAEG YN FFYNNU / A WALES OF VIBRANT CULTURE AND THRIVING WELSH LANGUAGE:
A society that promotes and protects culture, heritage and the Welsh language, and which encourages people to participate in the arts, and sports and recreation.
Employment relevance: Welsh Language Standards, bilingual workforce, cultural competency. The Welsh Government's target: 1 million Welsh speakers by 2050.

(7) CYMRU SY'N GYFRIFOL AR LEFEL FYD-EANG / A GLOBALLY RESPONSIBLE WALES:
A nation which, when doing anything to improve the economic, social, environmental and cultural well-being of Wales, takes account of whether doing so makes a positive contribution to global well-being.
Employment relevance: ethical supply chains, modern slavery, global workforce standards.

ALL SEVEN GOALS ARE INTERCONNECTED. A public body must work towards ALL of them, not pick and choose. Employment decisions affect multiple goals simultaneously — a decision about workforce restructuring touches Prosperous Wales (fair work), Healthier Wales (staff well-being), More Equal Wales (impact on disadvantaged groups), and Cohesive Communities (community impact).
sections.keyPrinciple: The 7 well-being goals and 5 ways of working are the LENS through which all Welsh public body decisions must be made. This is not a tick-box exercise — the Future Generations Commissioner and Auditor General examine whether public bodies are genuinely applying the sustainable development principle. The fair work amendment (October 2024) makes this Act directly relevant to employment law: securing fair work is now a statutory well-being goal for Wales.
refs: SPPP Act 2023 s.14 (fair work amendment, in force 1 October 2024)
UN Sustainable Development Goals (17 goals, linked to 7 Welsh goals)
Fair Work Commission Wales (2019) — 5 dimensions of fair work
Welsh Government — milestones (including 1 million Welsh speakers by 2050)
commonErrors: Not knowing that 'decent work' was replaced with 'FAIR WORK' in October 2024 — this is the most significant amendment to the WFG Act. Anyone advising Welsh public bodies on employment matters must reference 'fair work', not 'decent work'
Treating the 7 goals as a menu where you pick the ones that suit you — ALL 7 goals must be pursued. A decision that advances one goal at the expense of another is not sustainable development

Ignoring the 5 ways of working when making employment decisions — a workforce restructure that is decided without long-term thinking (hir dymor), prevention (atal), integration (integreiddio), collaboration (cydweithio), and involvement (cynnwys) does not comply with the sustainable development principle

Not understanding the intergenerational dimension — the sustainable development principle requires bodies to consider the needs of future generations. A short-term cost-cutting measure that degrades workforce capability for the next 20 years may fail this test

Treating the WFG Act as separate from employment law — since the fair work amendment, this Act is DIRECTLY relevant to employment decisions in Welsh public bodies. It is the statutory foundation for the SPPP Act's fair work obligations

versions.text: IN FORCE: well-being goals, sustainable development principle, 5 ways of working. AMENDED: 'decent work' replaced with 'fair work' in the Prosperous Wales goal (1 October 2024, SPPP Act s.14). The Act has been operational for 10 YEARS and is deeply embedded in Welsh public service governance.
versions.amendedBy: Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 s.14
versions.policyRationale: The Welsh Government's commitment to sustainable development — moving from a policy duty (Wales Act 2006 s.79) to a comprehensive statutory framework with goals, duties, and accountability mechanisms.

parts.title: Rhan 2 / Part 2 — Y Ddyletswydd Llesiant a'r Cyrff Cyhoeddus / The Well-being Duty and the Public Bodies (ss.3, 6-12)

sections.title: Y ddyletswydd ar gyrff cyhoeddus i gynnal datblygu cynaliadwy / The duty on public bodies to carry out sustainable development
sections.summary: The well-being duty is the OPERATIVE MECHANISM of the Act. Each listed public body must carry out sustainable development by: (a) setting and publishing well-being objectives designed to maximise its contribution to achieving each of the 7 well-being goals, and (b) taking all reasonable steps to meet those objectives. The duty applies to 44+ public bodies listed in s.6 — local authorities, NHS bodies, fire authorities, national bodies, and (since the SPPP Act) additional organisations.
sections.text:
Y DDYLETSWYDD LLESIANT / THE WELL-BEING DUTY (s.3):

Each public body must carry out sustainable development. This means:
(a) Setting and publishing WELL-BEING OBJECTIVES (amcanion llesiant) designed to maximise its contribution to achieving each of the 7 well-being goals.
(b) Taking ALL REASONABLE STEPS (in exercising its functions) to meet those objectives.

Objectives must be set within 12 months of a Senedd election and reviewed at least once during each Senedd term.

When setting objectives, the body must publish a STATEMENT explaining:
— WHY it believes the objectives will help achieve the well-being goals.
— HOW it has applied the sustainable development principle (5 ways of working).
— How it has involved people with an interest in achieving the goals.

Y CYRFF CYHOEDDUS / THE PUBLIC BODIES (s.6):

The well-being duty applies to the following bodies:

(a) GWEINIDOGION CYMRU / WELSH MINISTERS

(b) AWDURDODAU LLEOL / LOCAL AUTHORITIES (22):
All 22 Welsh local authorities (principal councils) — from Anglesey to Monmouthshire.

(c) BYRDDAU IECHYD LLEOL / LOCAL HEALTH BOARDS (7):
— Betsi Cadwaladr (North Wales)
— Hywel Dda (Mid and West Wales)
— Powys Teaching
— Aneurin Bevan (Gwent)
— Cardiff and Vale
— Cwm Taf Morgannwg (formerly Cwm Taf)
— Swansea Bay (formerly ABMU)

(d) YMDDIRIEDOLAETHAU'R GIG / NHS TRUSTS:
— Iechyd Cyhoeddus Cymru / Public Health Wales
— Ymddiriedolaeth GIG Felindre / Velindre NHS Trust
— Gwasanaeth Ambiwlans Cymru / Welsh Ambulance Services NHS Trust (added by SPPP Act)

(e) AWDURDODAU TÂN AC ACHUB / FIRE AND RESCUE AUTHORITIES (3):
— North Wales, Mid and West Wales, South Wales

(f) CYRFF CENEDLAETHOL / NATIONAL BODIES:
— Cyfoeth Naturiol Cymru / Natural Resources Wales
— Addysg a Gwella Iechyd Cymru / Health Education and Improvement Wales (HEIW)
— Iechyd a Gofal Digidol Cymru / Digital Health and Care Wales (added by SPPP Act)
— Cyngor Celfyddydau Cymru / Arts Council of Wales
— Chwaraeon Cymru / Sport Wales
— Llyfrgell Genedlaethol Cymru / National Library of Wales
— Amgueddfa Cymru / National Museum Wales
— Cyngor Cyllido Addysg Uwch Cymru / Higher Education Funding Council for Wales

(g) AWDURDODAU PARCIAU CENEDLAETHOL / NATIONAL PARK AUTHORITIES (3):
— Snowdonia (Eryri), Pembrokeshire Coast, Brecon Beacons (Bannau Brycheiniog)

ADRODD BLYNYDDOL / ANNUAL REPORTING (s.12):

Each public body must publish an annual report showing progress in meeting its well-being objectives. Where the Future Generations Commissioner has made recommendations, the body must publish its response — including an explanation if it does not follow a recommendation.

EMPLOYMENT RELEVANCE OF THE WELL-BEING DUTY:

For HR leaders in listed public bodies, the well-being duty means:

(a) WORKFORCE PLANNING must be aligned with well-being objectives. A workforce strategy that does not contribute to the 7 goals is not fulfilling the duty.

(b) RECRUITMENT must consider diversity (Involvement — cynnwys) and Welsh language capability (Vibrant Culture goal).

(c) PAY AND REWARD must contribute to fair work (Prosperous Wales goal, as amended).

(d) RESTRUCTURING AND REDUNDANCY must be assessed against all 7 goals and the 5 ways of working. A restructure that saves money (Prosperous) but damages staff well-being (Healthier), reduces Welsh language capability (Vibrant Culture), or fragments communities (Cohesive Communities) may not be sustainable development.

(e) PROCUREMENT must embed fair work (Prosperous Wales + SPPP Act). This flows from the well-being duty through the SPPP Act into procurement decisions and contract terms.

(f) SICKNESS ABSENCE AND WELL-BEING programmes must contribute to Healthier Wales. Prevention (atal) is one of the 5 ways of working — investing in prevention rather than managing crises.

For HR leaders in PRIVATE SECTOR companies contracting with Welsh public bodies:

The public body's well-being duty flows into procurement. When the public body sets well-being objectives, those objectives shape what it procures, how it procures, and what it requires from suppliers. A supplier who cannot demonstrate alignment with fair work, Welsh language, equality, and sustainability may find itself unable to compete for Welsh public contracts.
sections.keyPrinciple: The well-being duty is the mechanism that translates the Act's aspirational goals into operational obligations. Every listed public body must set well-being objectives, take all reasonable steps to meet them, and report annually on progress. Since October 2024, 'fair work' is one of the well-being goals — making the well-being duty DIRECTLY relevant to employment decisions. The duty does not just affect the public body itself — it shapes procurement decisions that affect the entire supply chain.
refs: SPPP Act 2023 — additional bodies added to s.6 list
Comisiynydd Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol / Future Generations Commissioner — recommendations and monitoring
Archwilydd Cyffredinol Cymru / Auditor General for Wales — examination powers (s.15)
commonErrors: Not knowing which bodies are listed under s.6 — if your organisation is on the list, you have a statutory well-being duty. Check the current list including SPPP Act additions
Setting well-being objectives that address only one or two goals — the duty requires objectives designed to maximise contribution to achieving EACH of the 7 goals. Cherry-picking is not compliance

Not involving staff and their representatives when setting well-being objectives — 'involvement' (cynnwys) is one of the 5 ways of working. For employment-related objectives, this means involving trade unions and staff representatives

Not publishing annual reports or not responding to Commissioner recommendations — the reporting requirements are not optional. The body must publish its response to Commissioner recommendations, including reasons for not following them

Not connecting the well-being duty to procurement decisions — the well-being duty drives what the public body procures and how. This is the link between the WFG Act and the SPPP Act's socially responsible procurement duties

parts.title: Rhan 3 / Part 3 — Y Comisiynydd, Byrddau Gwasanaethau Cyhoeddus, a Gorfodi / The Commissioner, Public Services Boards, and Accountability (ss.13-41)

sections.title: Comisiynydd Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol, Byrddau Gwasanaethau Cyhoeddus, a phwerau'r Archwilydd Cyffredinol / Future Generations Commissioner, Public Services Boards, and the Auditor General's powers
sections.summary: Part 3 establishes the Future Generations Commissioner (the 'guardian of future generations'), Part 4 establishes Public Services Boards for local well-being planning, and accountability is provided through the Commissioner's review powers and the Auditor General's examination powers. These mechanisms give the Act practical teeth — while enforcement is primarily through transparency, reporting, and soft power rather than penalties.
sections.text:
COMISIYNYDD CENEDLAETHAU'R DYFODOL CYMRU / FUTURE GENERATIONS COMMISSIONER FOR WALES (ss.13-23, Part 3):

The Commissioner's role is to act as a GUARDIAN for the interests of future generations in Wales. Current Commissioner: Derek Walker (appointed 2023, succeeding Sophie Howe who held the inaugural appointment 2016-2023).

(a) ADVISORY ROLE:
— Provide advice to public bodies and Public Services Boards.
— Promote and encourage public bodies to work towards the well-being goals.
— Carry out research into well-being goals, national indicators, and the sustainable development principle.

(b) REVIEW POWERS (s.20):
— Conduct reviews into how public bodies are taking account of the LONG-TERM IMPACT of their decisions.
— Make RECOMMENDATIONS based on findings.
— Public bodies must take ALL REASONABLE STEPS to follow recommendations.
— If the body does not follow a recommendation, it must EXPLAIN WHY and what alternative action it will take.

(c) FUTURE GENERATIONS REPORT:
— Published once per Senedd term (before a Senedd election).
— Contains the Commissioner's assessment of improvements public bodies should make.
— The 2025 report covers the period to 2025 and sets recommendations for the next Senedd term.

(d) ADVISORY PANEL:
— Supports the Commissioner.
— Includes the Children's Commissioner, Older People's Commissioner, Welsh Language Commissioner, Equalities and Human Rights Commission (Wales), Wales TUC representative, Welsh business representative, Chief Medical Officer, Natural Resources Wales representative.
— The inclusion of Wales TUC and business representatives reflects the social partnership model.

ARCHWILYDD CYFFREDINOL CYMRU / AUDITOR GENERAL FOR WALES (s.15):

The Auditor General examines public bodies to assess whether they have acted in accordance with the sustainable development principle when:
(a) Setting well-being objectives.
(b) Taking steps to meet those objectives.

The Auditor General works with the Future Generations Commissioner to coordinate examinations. Audit Wales reports are PUBLIC and can be highly critical. This creates transparency-based accountability.

BYRDDAU GWASANAETHAU CYHOEDDUS / PUBLIC SERVICES BOARDS (Part 4, ss.29-41):

A Public Services Board (PSB) is established for each local authority area in Wales (22 PSBs). Statutory members:
— The local authority.
— The local Health Board.
— The Fire and Rescue Authority.
— Natural Resources Wales.
— Invited participants include the Welsh Ministers, Chief Constable, Probation Service, and voluntary sector representatives.

PSBs must:
(a) Assess the state of LOCAL WELL-BEING in their area (Assessment of Local Well-being).
(b) Prepare and publish a LOCAL WELL-BEING PLAN setting objectives and steps.
(c) Publish annual reports showing progress.

Employment relevance: PSBs coordinate local workforce strategies, community resilience, and service delivery across multiple public bodies. An HR leader in a Welsh local authority should understand their PSB's local well-being plan and how workforce decisions contribute to it.

ACCOUNTABILITY AND ENFORCEMENT:

The WFG Act operates primarily through 'SOFT POWER' — transparency, reporting, recommendations, and public scrutiny rather than penalties or prosecution. The accountability mechanisms are:

(1) Commissioner reviews and recommendations — public bodies must respond.
(2) Auditor General examinations — public reports on compliance.
(3) Annual reporting — published progress against objectives.
(4) Future Generations Report — Senedd-term assessment.
(5) PSB reporting — local-level accountability.

Criticism: The Act has been criticised for lacking 'teeth' — there are no financial penalties for non-compliance, no power to compel changes, and no individual right of action for citizens. The Commissioner cannot ENFORCE compliance, only recommend and report.

Response: Supporters argue that the soft power model drives culture change more effectively than punitive enforcement. The Act has demonstrably changed how Welsh public bodies make decisions — well-being goals and the 5 ways of working are now embedded in strategic planning across the Welsh public sector. The SPPP Act's procurement provisions (commenced March 2026) add harder-edged obligations where fair work can be enforced through contract terms.

NATIONAL INDICATORS AND MILESTONES (s.10):

Welsh Ministers set 50 national indicators to measure progress towards the well-being goals. Key employment-related indicators include:
— Percentage of people in employment.
— Percentage of people in education, employment, or training (NEET target: 90% of 16-24 year olds by 2050).
— Gender pay gap.
— Percentage of people who are satisfied with their jobs.
— Median earnings.

Milestones include:
— 1 million Welsh speakers by 2050.
— Net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
— 90% of 16-24 year olds in education, employment, or training by 2050.

These long-term milestones demonstrate the INTERGENERATIONAL dimension — the Act is not about this year's KPIs but about the Wales of 2050.
sections.keyPrinciple: The WFG Act's accountability model is based on transparency and soft power, not penalties. The Commissioner reviews, recommends, and reports. The Auditor General examines. Public bodies publish. Citizens scrutinise. This model has driven significant culture change over 10 years but has been criticised for lacking enforcement teeth. The SPPP Act's procurement provisions (2026) complement this by adding contractual enforcement mechanisms for fair work.
refs: WFG Act 2015 ss.13-41 (Commissioner, Auditor General, PSBs)
Future Generations Commissioner for Wales — Derek Walker (2023-present)
22 Public Services Boards — Local Well-being Plans
commonErrors: Not knowing who the Future Generations Commissioner is or what their powers are — the Commissioner can review any listed public body's decisions and make recommendations that the body must respond to

Assuming the Act has no practical enforcement mechanism — while there are no penalties, the soft power model (Commissioner reviews, Auditor General examinations, public reporting) creates real accountability. Non-compliance is visible and reputationally damaging

Not connecting PSB local well-being plans to workforce decisions — the PSB's assessment of local well-being and its local well-being plan should inform workforce planning, recruitment, and service delivery in the local area

Ignoring the 50 national indicators and milestones — these are the measures by which Wales tracks progress. Employment-related indicators (NEET rates, gender pay gap, job satisfaction) are part of the framework

Treating the Commissioner's recommendations as optional — public bodies must take ALL REASONABLE STEPS to follow recommendations. If they do not follow a recommendation, they must explain why and what alternative action they will take. Simply ignoring a recommendation is not compliance

pendingAmendments.instrument: SPPP Act interaction — Social Partnership Duty exercised through WFG Act well-being objectives
pendingAmendments.status: The Social Partnership Duty (in force October 2024) requires consensus-seeking with trade unions when setting well-being objectives under this Act. The two Acts are now operationally inseparable.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — well-being objectives now have a social partnership dimension

pendingAmendments.status: The Commissioner's 2025 report covers the period to 2025 and sets recommendations for the next Senedd term. Monitor for recommendations relevant to fair work, workforce strategy, and employment practices.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — may drive changes in public body employment practices

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 interaction — UK-wide fair work and Welsh fair work
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 creates a UK-wide Fair Work Agency. Monitor for interaction between this and the Welsh WFG Act fair work goal and SPPP Act social partnership framework.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — potential for complementary or overlapping frameworks

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — mature framework (10 years), amended by SPPP Act (fair work goal, October 2024)
